questions.question: Your organization is moving to an agile way of work. You are concerned about how executives might misuse velocity metrics as a beating stick rather than as a tool for the team to understand their sustainable pace. Your team has hesitations about working in an agile environment. What should you do?
options: A. Add extra resources to your team to deliver faster and avoid executive involvement
B. Proceed with a predictive development approach and deal with the consequences later
C. Ask your team to work with agile velocity and iterations despite their hesitations
D. Suggest the team uses flow-based scheduling with a Kanban board for visual work management
questions.explanation: Flow-based scheduling with Kanban boards aligns with agile principles by using visual management and work-in-progress limits to maintain sustainable pace without forcing team adoption. This addresses both the velocity metric concern and team hesitations by providing an alternative agile approach that focuses on continuous flow rather than sprint-based velocity, minimizing waste such as context switching and handoffs.

questions.question: Your team has not worked on projects before and does not know about waterfall, agile, or other development models. Your project may also have requirements that are either certain or uncertain with varying levels of risk, and you are unsure which approach to use as you begin this new project. What will you do next?
options: A. Use waterfall and plan everything up front so the team has more stability
B. Use general phase definitions such as feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close
C. Use agile to ensure you can respond to change while your team's capability increases
D. Don't use any project approach; it doesn't matter as long as the work is getting done
questions.explanation: All projects go through the general phases of feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close regardless of the development life cycle chosen. Waterfall projects execute these phases sequentially once, while agile projects execute them multiple times for each feature or increment. This universal framework provides a stable starting point when requirements and approach are uncertain, allowing the team to understand the underlying principles regardless of the eventual methodology chosen.
questions.topic: Project Life Cycles, Phase Definitions, Development Approaches

questions.question: You are a project manager in an organization with a directive project management office and are currently between projects. The PMO asks you to prepare for an upcoming project in the accounting area by meeting with the functional manager. You have no team, no scope, no requirements, and no timeline as yet. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask the functional manager for their wish list of improvements so you can take these to your PMO
B. Create a project management plan so the project scope, budget, and schedule are more clear
C. Create a small backlog of work and start your first Sprint, then progressively update the backlog as the project progresses
D. Progressively elaborate the vision and create a vision statement or project charter to kick off the project
questions.explanation: Before initiating a project, you need to establish direction and purpose through a vision statement or project charter. This provides the foundation for all subsequent activities including scope definition, planning, backlog creation, and stakeholder engagement. Creating a management plan or backlog before establishing the vision and project charter is premature, as you lack the foundational direction needed to guide those efforts.
questions.topic: Project Initiation, Project Charter, Vision Statement
